findings.title: alert-state corruption fallback resets entire methodState to {} wiping 6200 tickers of accumulated baseline
findings.description: The 'Strip methodHistory from alert-state' step does `try { s = JSON.parse(readFileSync('alert-state.json')) } catch (e) { console.log('alert-state.json corrupt, resetting:', e.message); s = {}; }`. On any parse error — partial write from a prior killed run, a disk hiccup that yields invalid UTF-8, a runaway commit — the entire alert-state is silently replaced with `{}`. methodState (currently 6200 ticker baselines) is wiped, lastRun is null, fieldCoverage history is wiped. The replacement is then atomically written via tmp+rename and pushed to main via `git push` with concurrency:main-push protection. There is NO recovery path: the older valid state is overwritten on push, and detect-changes the NEXT run will see methodState={} and emit a wave of METHOD_PASS_GAINED INFO events for every single ticker × method pair (because every method is 'first-seen' again).
findings.mechanism: JSON.parse on a 21 MB file is fragile — any truncation, encoding issue, or trailing-garbage byte throws. The catch block silently degrades to total-reset. detect-changes.js (line 71-75) has identical fallback behaviour: `parsed = null; if (!parsed) parsed = {}`. Both paths lose state silently.
findings.reproducer: Append 1 byte of garbage to alert-state.json (`echo 'x' >> alert-state.json`). Run the workflow step manually. Observe `alert-state.json corrupt, resetting:` log line and the resulting file containing `{}`. On the next detect-changes run, every ticker × method emits METHOD_PASS_GAINED.
findings.impact: Silent data loss of ~30 days of accumulated pass-flip baselines. False-alarm flood (6200 tickers × ~30 methods = up to 186,000 spurious METHOD_PASS_GAINED events) on the run after recovery. Field-coverage history history wiped, so drift-detector cold-starts with no baseline and the 50%-floor rule becomes the only guardrail until a new 14-run history accrues.
findings.suggested_fix: On parse error, exit non-zero so the workflow halts instead of writing `{}` on top of (presumably) corrupt-but-recoverable state. Or: rename the corrupt file to alert-state.json.broken-<timestamp> and skip the step, preserving the most-recent valid push from origin/main. NEVER auto-reset committed state on a single parse failure — flag it and let a human intervene.

findings.title: fx-rates.json written non-atomically — kill during refresh-fx leaves corrupt FX table consumed by every downstream mcap conversion
findings.description: scripts/refresh-fx.js line 107: `fs.writeFileSync(outPath, JSON.stringify(out, null, 2));` writes directly to fx-rates.json. The step runs with `continue-on-error: true` in the workflow so failure is invisible. The file is consumed by pull-yahoo.js when normalizing non-USD market caps. A SIGKILL mid-write (the workflow has timeout-minutes:180 which can fire here, or the runner can OOM) yields a truncated fx-rates.json — JSON.parse fails on the next pull-yahoo run, the catch falls back to USD=1 only, and every non-USD ticker silently gets a wrong mcap (off by 1/FX-rate). That cascades into wrong sector medians, wrong mode picks, wrong walk-forward.
findings.mechanism: Pattern: read existing → mutate → write directly. No tmp+rename. The existing-read fallback already exists at lines 61-68 (catch returns USD=1 only), so a corrupted output is invisibly absorbed downstream.
findings.reproducer: Run `node scripts/refresh-fx.js` and SIGKILL it during the loop (any kill within the 200ms*~35 currencies window). fx-rates.json is left truncated. Next pull-yahoo run reads only USD=1.
findings.impact: Silent ~50% non-USD mcap corruption (every EUR/JPY/GBP ticker uses raw local-currency value as 'USD' market cap, which is order-of-magnitude wrong for JPY and similar). Mode picks (HYPERGROWTH, QUALITY_COMPOUNDER, TURNAROUND) get scrambled because they sort by mcap-derived metrics. Pull-yahoo currency normalization comments at Tag 124b assume fx-rates.json is always valid.
findings.suggested_fix: Use tmp+rename pattern (already used elsewhere e.g. detect-changes.js:114-116). Replace line 107 with: `const tmp = outPath + '.tmp.' + process.pid; fs.writeFileSync(tmp, JSON.stringify(out, null, 2)); fs.renameSync(tmp, outPath);`. Also add a sanity-check in pull-yahoo when loading fx-rates: if rates only contains USD=1, abort the non-USD pull leg with a clear error rather than silently using raw local values.

findings.title: sector-medians-auto.json, sector-medians-auto-legacy.json, sector-medians-rolling.json all written non-atomically
findings.description: writeAutoMedians (line 172, 181) and writeRollingMedians (line 231) all do `fs.writeFileSync(outPath, JSON.stringify(...))` directly. These three files are read on every method evaluation through _helpers.js loadAuto pattern (lines 90-129). If killed mid-write (the step uses `continue-on-error: true` so failure is invisible), the next pull-yahoo run reads a truncated medians file. _loadAutoMedians catches the parse error and sets cache to `{}` — meaning every method falls back to its hardcoded default threshold for the rest of the run. That can flip dozens of methods from PASS to FAIL or vice-versa, generating false METHOD_PASS_LOST events the next detect-changes run.
findings.mechanism: Three writes in sequence with no atomicity. Plus _loadAutoMedians/_loadRollingMedians both swallow parse errors silently (catch with no log).
findings.reproducer: SIGKILL during `node methods/sector-medians-compute.js ./snapshots` after the first writeFileSync completes but before the rolling-medians write. Next pull-yahoo run: every method using effectiveThreshold falls back to the static default, causing flip events.
findings.impact: Silent threshold-source regression on partial writes. Combined with the rolling-medians-merge logic (line 209: `merged = prior.medians || {}` after catch swallowing) a corrupt rolling file silently wipes 12-month accumulated history.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap all three writeFileSync calls in tmp+rename. Add WARN log inside the _loadAutoMedians / _loadRollingMedians catch blocks (currently silent).

findings.title: generate-methods-report.js still reads methodHistory from alert-state.json — silently empty after sidecar migration
findings.description: After the Tag 134 Phase 5.2 / F-SM-007 migration, methodHistory lives in method-history-state.json (sidecar) and is explicitly stripped from alert-state.json by the workflow's 'Strip methodHistory' step (line 291: `delete s.methodHistory`). However generate-methods-report.js line 37 still does `methodHistory = s.methodHistory || {}` from alert-state.json. Therefore trend signals (`Trend.computeTrend(tickerHist[m.id] || [], m.thresholdOp)`) always run on `[]` for every ticker × method — every trend is 'n/a' (need >= 3 history datapoints, see methods/trend.js line 21).
findings.mechanism: File reads alert-state.json instead of method-history-state.json. The strip step ALWAYS removes methodHistory from alert-state, even if detect-changes (for some reason) wrote it inline. So this code path is permanently broken.
findings.reproducer: Run `node generate-methods-report.js` against the current committed state. Inspect the generated HTML — every method × ticker trend column shows 'n/a'. Add a console.log of methodHistory keys count immediately after line 37: it is 0.
findings.impact: Methods Report (deprecated per .gitignore line 10 but still in the codebase) shows zero trend information. If anyone re-enables generate-methods-report in the workflow they will get a regressed dashboard with no trend column. Low blast radius today (file is gitignored, not invoked in workflow) but a latent bug waiting to surface.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the read with the sidecar: `try { const p = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync('./method-history-state.json', 'utf8')); methodHistory = (p && p.methodHistory) || {}; } catch (e) {}`. Or import the loader from detect-changes.js.

findings.title: method-effectiveness-cache.json (outputs/) lives in gitignored dir — every fresh CI run starts with an empty cache, but methods-history older than 7d is already archived/deleted
findings.description: method-effectiveness.js stores its per-vintage cache at `outputs/method-effectiveness-cache.json` (line 38). The .gitignore line 39 ignores `outputs/` entirely (except outputs/.gitkeep). Every CI run gets a fresh clone with NO cache. The script then needs to re-read every methods-history vintage to populate. BUT scripts/archive-old-snapshots.js deletes methods-history/*.json files older than 7 days (--methods-keep-days 7 in workflow line 274). So on a fresh runner, method-effectiveness can ONLY use the latest 7 vintages — older ones were archived to external-data/methods-history-archive/ (also gitignored, line 25) and the cache that would have preserved them is also gitignored. Bootstrap CI computations require MIN_VINTAGES=4 vintages per method × horizon group — for 84d horizon this needs vintages at least 84+1 days old, which were deleted. Net effect: 84d alpha is permanently uncomputable in CI.
findings.mechanism: Three-way gitignore: cache (outputs/), archive (external-data/methods-history-archive/), and originals (methods-history/, only last 7 days kept). Together they form a 'memory hole' for any backtest horizon longer than the keep-days window.
findings.reproducer: On a fresh clone: `node scripts/method-effectiveness.js`. Inspect outputs/method-effectiveness.json — every 84d entry has `evidenceGate: 'insufficient-vintages-need-4'`. Even after 4 daily runs, only the 28d horizon has enough data because 84d requires vintages 84d+ old which were never present.
findings.impact: method-effectiveness audit can never validate the 12-week (84d) horizon in CI. Methodology Report combining walk-forward + method-effectiveness shows zero longer-horizon evidence. This silently undercuts the core 'is this method earning its weight' measurement (workflow comment line 184).
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) commit the cache (un-gitignore outputs/method-effectiveness-cache.json specifically — it is small, append-only, and the script handles corruption), or (b) have archive-old-snapshots.js also read into the cache before deleting (write-through), or (c) read directly from the NDJSON archive when a vintage isn't in methods-history/. Option (a) is the simplest correct fix.

findings.title: method-effectiveness-cache.json grows unbounded — never pruned, never atomic write
findings.description: Line 207 `fs.writeFileSync(CACHE_PATH, JSON.stringify(cache));` is non-atomic. The cache stores `vintageReturns[date|horizonKey] = entries[]` keyed by every historical vintage × horizon ever processed. There is NO pruning: even after the source methods-history file is archived and deleted, the cache retains its entries forever. With 2 horizons (28d, 84d) and ~3000-12000 stocks × ~30 methods per vintage, a single vintage easily produces 100k+ entries. Over a year of daily runs the cache will cross 100 MB. Not a 50 MB GitHub limit problem today (outputs/ is gitignored), but combined with the F-SM-019 fix to commit it would become one within months.
findings.mechanism: Unbounded append; no retention policy; non-atomic write.
findings.reproducer: Inspect outputs/method-effectiveness-cache.json size growth over time on a long-running runner. Expected: linear in (days × horizons × stocks × methods).
findings.impact: Memory pressure: JSON.parse of the entire cache file at script startup grows proportionally. Eventually OOMs the runner on large repos. If F-SM-019 fix is applied without retention, committed file size will become the bottleneck.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a retention pass at start of main(): drop cache entries where the date < today - max(HORIZONS_DAYS) - 30. Use tmp+rename for the write. The fix is essentially: `for (const k of Object.keys(cache.vintageReturns)) { const date = k.split('|')[0]; if (date < retentionCutoff) delete cache.vintageReturns[k]; }`.

findings.title: watchlist.json written non-atomically by refresh-universe.js and prune-watchlist.js — kill mid-write loses entire universe
findings.description: Both scripts directly `fs.writeFileSync(args.watchlist, JSON.stringify(wl, null, 2))` after rebuilding the universe in memory. The watchlist.json file is the SOLE source of truth for every subsequent step (pull-yahoo, etc). A SIGKILL during the JSON.stringify of a 13000-element array yields a truncated watchlist.json. Next pull-yahoo run reads it, JSON.parse throws, and the entire pull halts (or worse, partial parse). Both refresh-universe and prune-watchlist run under `continue-on-error: true` in the workflow, so failure is invisible.
findings.mechanism: Direct write to production file. No tmp+rename. No backup.
findings.reproducer: SIGKILL during `node refresh-universe.js --watchlist watchlist.json` after the JSON.stringify has begun but before the writeFileSync returns. Next read of watchlist.json fails parse.
findings.impact: Single corruption event halts the entire daily pipeline (pull-yahoo aborts on watchlist parse error). Has to be manually recovered from git history.
findings.suggested_fix: Tmp+rename pattern in both scripts. refresh-universe.js:327 should be: `const tmp = args.out + '.tmp.' + process.pid; fs.writeFileSync(tmp, JSON.stringify(wlRaw, null, 2)); fs.renameSync(tmp, args.out);`. Same for prune-watchlist.js:156.

findings.title: _loadAutoMedians / _loadRollingMedians / _loadSectorMedians swallow JSON.parse errors silently (cache := {})
findings.description: All three load helpers do `try { ... = JSON.parse(...) } catch (e) { ... = {} }`. No warning, no error log. When sector-medians-auto.json is partially-written or corrupted, every method silently falls back to its hardcoded default threshold for the rest of the run. The same loaders memoize the empty result for the process lifetime (`if (_autoMediansCache !== null) return _autoMediansCache`), so even if the file is fixed mid-run, the script keeps using {}. The combination of (a) silent fallback and (b) memoization makes corruption invisible to the operator and persistent within a run.
findings.mechanism: Catch with empty body. The Tag 134/167 rolling/regional precedence assumes the files exist and are valid — when they aren't, every method silently regresses to its lowest-priority fallback.
findings.reproducer: Corrupt methods/sector-medians-auto.json by truncation. Run any method evaluation. Observe NO log line indicating fallback was triggered. Compare pass-counts vs prior run — drastically different.
findings.impact: Compounds with F-SM-017: any partial write to a medians file triggers silent fallback. Operator sees a wave of method flip events the next detect-changes run and has no diagnostic trail.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `console.warn('_loadAutoMedians: parse failed (' + e.message + '), falling back to {}.');` inside each catch. Also: consider clearing the cache after, say, 5 minutes so a mid-run fix can be picked up.

findings.title: pull-sec-xbrl manifest written non-atomically — committed file (external-data/sec-xbrl/_manifest.json) can be corrupted by SIGKILL
findings.description: Two writes to MANIFEST_PATH (line 31: external-data/sec-xbrl/_manifest.json). Per .gitignore line 22 this file IS committed (the per-CIK JSON files are gitignored, only the manifest is committed). Both writes (progress checkpoint at line 133, final at line 140) are direct fs.writeFileSync — no tmp+rename. The progress write happens every 100 iterations, so the corruption window is small per iteration but compounded across a long pull.
findings.mechanism: Direct write. The manifest contains per-CIK { fetchedAt, lastError, etag, ... } and is the de-dup state for SEC pulls (90-day STALE_DAYS gate at line 34). Corruption here re-pulls every CIK from SEC, blowing through the 10 req/sec rate limit and risking IP block.
findings.reproducer: SIGKILL during pull-sec-xbrl mid-write. Next run: JSON.parse fails on manifest (no catch — script reads at the top, would crash). Even worse: the corrupted file gets committed and pushed to main on the monthly-sec-xbrl workflow.
findings.impact: Re-pulling all 8000+ CIKs at 8 req/sec = ~17 minutes that should be 1-2 minutes. Risk of SEC IP block. Committed corruption needs manual revert from git history.
findings.suggested_fix: Use tmp+rename for both writes. Wrap the read-at-start in try/catch with explicit error log if parse fails.

findings.title: writeRollingMedians silently wipes 12-month rolling history on parse error of prior file
findings.description: Line 205-208: `let prior = { medians: {} }; if (fs.existsSync(outPath)) { try { prior = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(outPath, 'utf8')) || { medians: {} }; } catch (e) {} }`. If sector-medians-rolling.json is corrupt (combined with F-SM-017 non-atomic write), the catch block silently leaves prior at its initial `{ medians: {} }` — 12 months of rolling history is wiped on the very next save. No warning, no log, no error.
findings.mechanism: Catch with empty body resets prior to default. Subsequent merged at line 209 inherits the empty default. Final write at line 231 commits the wiped state.
findings.reproducer: Corrupt methods/sector-medians-rolling.json (truncate by 100 bytes). Run `node methods/sector-medians-compute.js ./snapshots`. After the run, inspect the file — only today's entry survives.
findings.impact: 12-month rolling medians (ROLLING_MIN_WEEKS=12 in _helpers.js) take 84 days to re-mature after a single corruption event. During that time, effectiveThreshold falls back from rolling → auto → static. Threshold sources change silently, causing PASS/FAIL flips invisibly attributed to 'methodology drift'.
findings.suggested_fix: Same fix as F-SM-022: log a warning in the catch. Also: keep a `.bak` of the prior file when a parse error occurs so the operator can recover.

findings.title: archive-old-snapshots verify check only reads first line — bad appended data not detected before originals are deleted
findings.description: F-SM-013 ostensibly added a 'verify the archive is readable before unlinking originals' check (lines 83-92). But the check only reads and parses the FIRST line of the NDJSON file: `const firstLine = fs.readFileSync(ndjsonPath, 'utf8').split('\n')[0]; ... JSON.parse(firstLine);`. When the file exists from a prior run, `appendFileSync` adds new content at the end. The first line is the OLD valid line from the prior run, so the verify passes. Any corruption in the newly-appended content is invisible. Then originals are unlinked (line 94-97) — data lost.
findings.mechanism: First-line-only verification cannot detect tail corruption in an append-mode NDJSON. Should at minimum verify the LAST line that was appended.
findings.reproducer: Pre-populate external-data/methods-history-archive/2026-04.ndjson with one valid NDJSON line. Then simulate a partial-write append (truncate the new content to a non-JSON-ending byte). Run archive-old-snapshots. Verify passes (first line is good). Originals deleted. Try to replay the archive — the appended chunk is unparseable.
findings.impact: Silent data loss of 1-30 days of methods-history or picks-history per month-boundary corruption event. Affects backtest reproducibility — replaying the archive yields a partial set.
findings.suggested_fix: Read the entire file or at least all lines added this run and parse each. Cheaper: build the NDJSON content in memory, write to a `.tmp` file with full content (existing + new), verify by line-by-line parse, then atomic rename.

findings.title: archive-old-snapshots JSON.stringify spread can drop the date prefix if content has a 'date' field
findings.description: Line 72: `const lines = entries.map(e => JSON.stringify({ date: e.date, ...e.content })).join('\n') + '\n';`. The object literal `{ date: e.date, ...e.content }` spreads e.content AFTER `date`, so if e.content itself has a `date` field (which it does for both methods-history files — line 49 of snapshot-methods-history.js sets `data.date = today` at the top level — and picks-history files which have `asOf` but no `date`), the spread OVERRIDES the prefix.date with e.content.date. For methods-history this is technically the same value but for picks-history it's not present, so all good for picks. For methods-history: `data.date` is set at write time to ISO `today` (e.g. '2026-05-15'). The archive `e.date` is derived from the FILENAME ('2026-05-15'). They match — but only because both are produced under the same UTC timezone. A workflow that ran late at night UTC could create a methods-history file where filename and inner `date` differ by a day. The spread would silently use the inner date, breaking replay-by-filename.
findings.mechanism: Object literal property order: later keys override earlier ones. The spread is intentional but does not anticipate that content may carry the same key.
findings.reproducer: Create methods-history/2026-05-14.json with `{ date: '2026-05-15', stocks: {...} }` (mismatch). Run archive-old-snapshots. The archive line will read `date: '2026-05-15'` even though the source filename was '2026-05-14'.
findings.impact: Date attribution drift in archive replay. Minor today but compounds with any tooling that joins archived data back to filename-based date.
findings.suggested_fix: Reverse the spread: `JSON.stringify({ ...e.content, date: e.date })` so the filename-derived date always wins. Or pick one source of truth explicitly.

findings.title: _saveFirstSeenCache (picks-history/_first-seen.json) written non-atomically and ticker entries never expire
findings.description: Two issues: (1) Line 141 `fs.writeFileSync(cachePath, JSON.stringify(cache))` is non-atomic. picks-history/_first-seen.json IS committed (picks-history/ is NOT gitignored). A SIGKILL during snapshot-picks leaves the file corrupted; _loadFirstSeenCache catches the parse error silently and rebuilds from all vintages. The rebuild is slow but functional. The atomicity is still a write-safety issue because the corrupted file gets committed and pushed. (2) The cache only ADDS tickers (line 278: `if (!firstSeenMap[modeId][p.ticker]) firstSeenMap[modeId][p.ticker] = ...`). A ticker that was a HYPERGROWTH pick once in 2025 stays in the cache forever even if it's been delisted or out-of-picks for a year. The cache grows monotonically. At 7 KB today this isn't an immediate threat but it's unbounded.
findings.mechanism: Non-atomic write + monotonic accumulation. The committed file silently grows; the only pruning is the _buildFirstSeenMap fallback that reads from picks-history/ files (which IS pruned by archive to 90 days), but the cache check at line 148 prefers cached data and never rebuilds once a cache exists.
findings.reproducer: (1) SIGKILL snapshot-picks during the write. Next run: _loadFirstSeenCache catches and rebuilds — slow but works. The corrupted file gets committed in between. (2) Long-term: inspect _first-seen.json after 1+ year. Compare its ticker list to current picks-history files — there will be hundreds of stale tickers.
findings.impact: Committed corrupt file in git history. Long-term cache bloat (~7 KB grows to ~50 KB / year of operation). weeksOnList computation pulls firstSeenAt for tickers that may have been out of picks for months, misleading the UI.
findings.suggested_fix: Tmp+rename for the write. Add a pruning pass: drop tickers from cache whose firstSeenAt > 365 days ago AND not present in the current run's picks.

findings.title: snapshot-picks latest.json + vintage.json written non-atomically — kill mid-write yields corrupt latest pointer
findings.description: Lines 269-271: `fs.writeFileSync(outFile, JSON.stringify(result, null, 2)); ... fs.writeFileSync(path.join(args.out, 'latest.json'), JSON.stringify(result, null, 2));`. Both are committed (picks-history/ is committed). snapshot-methods-history.js was fixed (F-SM-005) to use tmp+rename (lines 122-124) but snapshot-picks was not. The picks-history/YYYY-MM-DD.json is the SOLE input to walk-forward-perf for vintage analysis. A corrupted vintage file silently kills that vintage's contribution. The 'latest.json' is read by dashboard JS — corruption means broken UI.
findings.mechanism: Direct write, no tmp+rename. The step is workflow-`continue-on-error: true` so failure is invisible.
findings.reproducer: SIGKILL during snapshot-picks output write. Next walk-forward-perf run skips this vintage; dashboard fetches latest.json and gets a JSON parse error in the browser.
findings.impact: Vintage continuity gap (workflow comment line 164 explicitly says 'a silent gap of even one week destroys vintage continuity'). UI breakage. Tag 134 picks-history is committed precisely because it is mission-critical — but the writes that produce it lack the atomicity it deserves.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the same tmp+rename pattern as snapshot-methods-history.js:122-124 to both writes.

findings.title: concurrency:main-push protects pushes, but workflow_dispatch + scheduled cron can still overlap LOCAL state mutations on the runner
findings.description: concurrency: group: main-push, cancel-in-progress: false serializes the PUSH step but does NOT prevent two workflow runs from executing in parallel on different runners. If Karl triggers a workflow_dispatch while the scheduled 2 AM run is in flight, both runs do checkout, refresh-fx, pull-yahoo, etc. independently on separate runners — there's no shared filesystem race because each runner has its own disk. But: both will commit and push at the end. The concurrency group queues the second push. When the second push runs, its working tree was based on the FIRST run's pre-push origin/main, so the rebase --strategy-option theirs (line 315) discards the first run's data in favor of the second run's. This is documented as 'last-writer-wins' but means the FIRST run's work is silently lost — including its alert-state.json methodState mutations and its fieldCoverage.history append.
findings.mechanism: concurrency:main-push only serializes pushes, not full runs. Two parallel runs both produce alert-state.json mutations and both want to push. The second-completing run's push wins because of --strategy-option theirs, discarding the first run's state delta.
findings.reproducer: Trigger workflow_dispatch at 2:05 AM (5 min after the scheduled run starts). Both runs proceed. The first finishes, pushes. The second finishes, rebases with theirs (taking ITS own files), and pushes. Diff the final alert-state.json against the first run's output: the first run's methodState/fieldCoverage delta is gone.
findings.impact: Manual reruns silently overwrite scheduled-run state. Field-coverage history skips an entry (which may trigger MIN_HISTORY_FOR_ALERT=2 cold-start behaviour again). methodState lastChanged dates skip a day, weakening the 30-day pruning window logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) set cancel-in-progress: true so a manual dispatch cancels the in-flight scheduled run (cleaner semantics: 'I want this fresh data, abandon the other'), or (b) before push, fetch + replay our state-merge logic instead of --strategy-option theirs. Option (a) is the trivial fix; option (b) requires building a state-merger.

findings.title: method-history-state.json (sidecar) grows unbounded — no per-ticker pruning when ticker disappears from universe
findings.description: saveState() at lines 99-104 prunes methodState entries for tickers absent from the current run if their lastChanged is > 30 days old. methodHistory has NO equivalent pruning. Line 218-219 deep-clones the full methodHistory at the start of every run; line 253 only overwrites tickers present in the current snapshots. A ticker that was delisted 6 months ago retains its full 8-entry history in the sidecar forever. The sidecar is 8.9 MB today (8000+ tickers × ~30 methods × up to 8 history entries each).
findings.mechanism: Asymmetric pruning: methodState has it, methodHistory doesn't. Methods/_helpers.js memoization isn't relevant here (different file), but the same 'unbounded growth' risk applies.
findings.reproducer: Run detect-changes for 100 cycles with a stable universe, then for 1 cycle with watchlist shrunk by 50%. Inspect method-history-state.json: it still has entries for all the dropped tickers.
findings.impact: Sidecar bloat (committed: 8.9 MB and growing). Approaches GitHub 50 MB committed-file warning over 12+ months. Once it crosses 100 MB, push will be rejected entirely.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the 30-day pruning logic from methodState into methodHistory: for each ticker in methodHistory not in current snapshots, check if the most-recent history entry across all methods is older than e.g. 60 days; if so, drop the ticker.

findings.title: alert-state.json methodState deep-clone (line 218) is wasteful — re-uses a 6200-ticker × 30-method object that will be partially overwritten
findings.description: `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(state.methodState || {}))` deep-clones the entire methodState (currently 6200 tickers, each with ~30 methods, total ~21 MB of structured data). The clone is then partially overwritten by the per-ticker loop. The clone is needed to preserve dropped-ticker state (per F-SM-014 comment), but the cost is ~150-300ms of JSON.stringify+parse for each detect-changes run.
findings.mechanism: Naive deep-clone via JSON round-trip. Most cloned subtrees are immediately overwritten.
findings.reproducer: Time `node detect-changes.js`: ~300-500ms of CPU is in JSON serialization at lines 218-219.
findings.impact: Minor perf hit (~0.5 sec) per run. Not blocking but unnecessary.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace deep-clone with shallow-clone of the outer object only: `methodState: Object.assign({}, state.methodState || {})`. Per-ticker mutations always create a NEW object at line 242 (`newState.methodState[ticker] = tickerNewState`), so shallow is sufficient. Saves ~300ms per run.

findings.title: fieldCoverage.history truncation warning fires every run after the WINDOW=14 limit is reached
findings.description: F-SM-010 added a warning when history is truncated. But once the history reaches the steady-state size of HISTORY_WINDOW=14, EVERY subsequent run triggers the warning (extended.length=15 > 14 truncates to 14). The intent was to warn on WINDOW config changes, but the implementation fires on the routine append→slice path. The warning becomes noise in every workflow log.
findings.mechanism: extended = [...safe, newEntry] is always one longer than safe. Once safe is at WINDOW size, extended is WINDOW+1, triggering the warn. The warning fires for the EXPECTED steady-state operation.
findings.reproducer: After ~14 detect-changes runs, every subsequent log will contain `fieldCoverage: history truncated from 15 to 14`. Search the workflow log — it should appear every run currently.
findings.impact: Log noise; obscures real warnings. No data integrity issue.
findings.suggested_fix: Only warn when the original safe.length was already > HISTORY_WINDOW (true config-change case): `if (safe.length > HISTORY_WINDOW) console.warn(...)`. Or remove the warning entirely.
